calendar.matters:
Notice Of Motion For Peremptory Writ Of Administrative And Or Ordinary Mandamus; Memo Of Points And Authorities; Request For Costs; Declaration Of Counsel Request For Statement Of Decision; Administrative Record Lodged
Off Calendar Nov-10-2021 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE:
Ellen Mendelson, Esq. (415.585.0815; ellenlaw@aol.com) for Petitioner.
Kelly Collins, Esq. (415.554.3914; kelly.collins@sfcityatty.org) Deputy City Attorney for Respondents.

ARGUED (as fully reported in the reporter's transcript). The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

PETITIONER STEPHEN ZIMMERMAN'S Motion For Peremptory Writ Of Administrative And Or Ordinary Mandamus:

Petitioner Zimmerman's petition for writ of mandate is denied. The Court employs the independent judgment rule because this case involves vested retirement benefits. (See Dickey v. Retirement Bd. (1976) 16 Cal.3d 745, 751.)

Petitioner's contention that respondent did not contribute to his retirement allowance lacks merit. San Francisco Charter sec. A8.597-11(a) provides that a member, such as petitioner, shall "provide part of the retirement allowance." (emphasis added.) Subsection (b) makes it clear that the City contributes to petitioner's allowance. It provides: "The City and County shall contribute to the Retirement System such amounts as may be necessary, when added to the contributions referred to in Subsection (a)" Respondent presented a spreadsheet on remand that documented its contributions and how the offset was calculated. (ARAR 77-79.)

Petitioner's argument that respondent cannot claw back pre-retirement workers' compensation benefits and violated Labor Code section 3571 also lacks merit. In Symington v. City of Albany (1971) 5 Cal.3d 23, the court makes it clear that respondent is entitled to offset a retiree's disability retirement allowance to cover the cost of workers' compensation benefits as long as the amount of the offset is proportional to the respondent's contribution to the employee's pension. Petitioner is not entitled to double recovery.

Petitioner fails to demonstrate that the recalculation of the offset in the amount of $77,102.77 was erroneous. "The findings of the board come before the court with a strong presumption of their correctness, and the burden rests on the complaining party to convince the court that the board's decision is contrary to the weight of the evidence." (Fukuda v. City of Angels (1999) 20 Cal.4th 805, 812.) Petitioner fails to meet that burden.

Petitioner's remaining substantive arguments are an improper attempt to re-litigate issues that have already been ruled upon in Petitioner's original action and are outside the scope of the limited remand the Court ordered in its December 2, 2020 statement of decision in that action (No. CPF-20-517075). Petitioner's request for fees is denied. Respondent shall prepare a statement of decision in compliance with Code Civ. Proc. section 632.

Statement of Decision is signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: S. Le, Court Reporter: (Eric Throne, CSR No. 7855, Email: eric.throne.csr@gmail.com, Tel: 707-305-8964) =(302/EPS)
